_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against NICE CKS Vitiligo, JAMA Dermatology consensus guidelines, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Vitiligo: Types, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment Options
heroOverview: Vitiligo is an autoimmune skin disorder characterized by the progressive loss of melanocytes — the pigment-producing cells — resulting in well-demarcated, chalk-white depigmented patches on the skin. The condition affects approximately 1% of the global population across all ethnicities and ages, though it is particularly noticeable in individuals with darker skin tones. The pathophysiology involves both genetic predisposition and autoimmune destruction of melanocytes, with recent advances in understanding highlighting the critical role of the innate and adaptive immune system. While traditionally considered purely cosmetic, vitiligo significantly impacts psychological well-being and quality of life, and modern treatment approaches — including topical agents, light therapy, systemic immunosuppression, and surgical melanocyte transfer — can achieve repigmentation in the majority of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1% of the global population; affects all ethnicities and both sexes equally, though more psychologically impactful in darker-skinned individuals
keyStats.demographics: Onset before age 30 in 50% of patients; peak onset in the second and third decades of life; affects men and women equally
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of onset is 20-24 years for non-segmental vitiligo; segmental vitiligo typically appears before age 10

definition: Vitiligo (ICD-10: L80) is an acquired depigmentation disorder resulting from the destruction of epidermal and/or hair follicle melanocytes. The loss of melanocytes leads to complete absence of melanin in affected areas, producing chalk-white macules and patches with well-defined, often hyperpigmented borders. The condition is classified into two main clinical forms: non-segmental vitiligo (NSV, the most common form, involving bilateral symmetric depigmentation) and segmental vitiligo (unilateral dermatomal distribution, typically stabilized after 6-12 months). The pathophysiology is multifactorial, involving genetic susceptibility, autoimmune-mediated melanocyte destruction, oxidative stress, and neural abnormalities. Vitiligo is associated with other autoimmune conditions including autoimmune thyroid disease, type 1 diabetes, and pernicious anemia — the presence of these comorbidities should be screened for at diagnosis.

typesClassification.description: The most common form, accounting for 80-90% of cases. Characterized by bilateral, symmetric depigmented patches with a characteristic distribution pattern. Subtypes include: generalized (widespread, all body areas), acrofacial (hands and face), mucosal (lips and genitals), and universal (near-total body depigmentation, rare). Progression is unpredictable and typically follows a relapsing-remitting course.

Unilateral depigmentation following a dermatomal or quasi-dermatomal distribution, typically in a single segment of the body. Appears earlier in life than NSV (often before age 10), progresses rapidly for 6-12 months, then stabilizes permanently. Less associated with other autoimmune conditions than NSV. Hair in affected areas may also depigment (leukotrichia).
A rare combination of segmental and non-segmental patterns in the same patient, where segmental vitiligo appears first followed by later development of non-segmental patches.

Isolated depigmentation of mucous membranes (lips, genitalia, oral mucosa) or the lips and fingertips only, without sufficient criteria to classify as NSV or segmental. May represent early-stage disease or a distinct subtype.

The rarest variant, involving depigmentation of more than 80% of the body surface area. Often evolves from generalized NSV over many years. When extensive, some patients elect total depigmentation of remaining pigmented skin for a uniform appearance.

primarySymptoms: Depigmented chalk-white patches — the hallmark of vitiligo. These are completely devoid of pigment, with well-defined borders that may be slightly raised or hyperpigmented relative to surrounding skin.

Symmetric distribution (non-segmental type) — bilateral patches typically affecting the face, neck, axillae, groin, and extensor surfaces of the hands and feet. The periorificial areas (around the eyes, nostrils, mouth, umbilicus) are commonly affected.

Unilateral band-like distribution (segmental type) — depigmentation following a dermatomal or blaschkoid pattern on one side of the body, typically in the trigeminal nerve distribution or a truncal dermatome.
Koebner phenomenon — new depigmented patches appearing at sites of skin trauma, friction, pressure, or cuts. This is important for patient counseling regarding lifestyle and surgical procedures.
Premature hair graying or leukotrichia — affected scalp hair, eyebrows, eyelashes, or beard hair may turn white, indicating melanocyte loss from hair follicles. This affects repigmentation prognosis.
Mucosal involvement — depigmentation of the lips, oral mucosa, and genitalia is common, particularly in the acrofacial and mucosal subtypes of NSV.
Absence of scaling, inflammation, or sensory changes — unlike many other skin conditions, vitiligo patches are asymptomatic (no itching or pain) and have normal skin texture.
earlyWarningSigns: New chalk-white patch on the skin — particularly on the face, hands, feet, or around body orifices, especially if the patch is completely devoid of pigment and well-demarcated
Loss of pigment following a skin injury — cuts, scrapes, burns, or friction points developing depigmented patches weeks to months later (Koebner phenomenon)
Premature graying of hair — in individuals under 30, particularly if the graying is patchy rather than diffuse
Rapidly progressive depigmentation — patches spreading over weeks to months, particularly in children and young adults
Asymmetric or unilateral patches in a young child — suggestive of segmental vitiligo, which benefits from early intervention
emergencySigns: Sudden widespread depigmentation with constitutional symptoms — unusual and should prompt evaluation for other conditions (leukoderma from chemicals, guttate hypomelanosis, or post-inflammatory hypopigmentation)

Vitiligo with concurrent autoimmune symptoms — signs of thyroid disease (fatigue, weight change, temperature intolerance), polyuria/polydipsia (diabetes), or pallor (pernicious anemia) warrant urgent medical evaluation
Ocular symptoms in vitiligo patients — some patients with ocular vitiligo have uveitis or retinal changes; eye symptoms warrant ophthalmology referral
Significant psychological distress with suicidal ideation — vitiligo causes severe psychological burden; patients with depression or suicidal thoughts require urgent mental health support

causes.description: The primary pathophysiologic mechanism in non-segmental vitiligo. CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes recognize and destroy melanocytes expressing specific antigens (tyrosinase, TRP-1, TRP-2, gp100, Melan-A/MART-1). Circulating melanocyte-specific antibodies are detected in many patients. This autoimmune process is driven by IFN-gamma and a Th1-skewed immune response, with recent work identifying the JAK-STAT pathway as a critical signaling axis.

Vitiligo is a polygenic disorder with significant heritable component. Over 40 genetic loci have been associated with vitiligo risk, including HLA alleles (HLA-A*02:01, HLA-DRB1*04:04), immune regulatory genes (PTPN22, CTLA4, IL2RA), and melanocyte-related genes (MC1R, OCA2, TYR). First-degree relatives of affected individuals have a 6-22% lifetime risk compared to 0.14% in the general population.

Melanocytes in vitiligo-affected skin show evidence of severe oxidative stress — elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS), decreased glutathione, reduced catalase activity, and accumulation of hydrogen peroxide. This oxidative burden damages melanocytes and generates neoantigens that trigger autoimmune responses. The vitiligo microenvironment is pro-oxidant, creating a feedforward loop of melanocyte vulnerability and immune activation.

A neurochemical mediator (norepinephrine) released from sympathetic nerve endings may be toxic to melanocytes. This theory is supported by the segmental distribution in some cases and the observation that vitiligo patches have increased neural density. This mechanism may be particularly relevant for segmental vitiligo.

Defective melanocyte-keratinocyte adhesion, mediated by E-cadherin and integrins, may cause melanocyte fragility and detachment. Keratinocytes from vitiligo patients show abnormal signaling and reduced support for melanocyte survival in culture.

Vitiligo develops or spreads in response to specific triggers in genetically predisposed individuals: skin trauma (Koebner phenomenon), sunburn, emotional stress, chemical exposure (phenolic compounds in adhesives, dyes, sanitizers), pregnancy, and systemic illness. These triggers do not cause vitiligo alone but unmask it in susceptible individuals.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of vitiligo or other autoimmune disease
riskFactors.description: Having a first-degree relative with vitiligo increases lifetime risk approximately 10-fold. Family members of vitiligo patients have elevated rates of autoimmune thyroid disease, type 1 diabetes, alopecia areata, and pernicious anemia.

Autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's, Graves'), type 1 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, and pernicious anemia share genetic and mechanistic overlap with vitiligo. Patients with one autoimmune condition are at increased risk for developing others.

riskFactors.factor: Personal or family history of atopy
riskFactors.description: Atopic dermatitis, asthma, and allergic rhinitis are more common in vitiligo patients and their families, suggesting shared genetic susceptibility for atopic and autoimmune conditions.

Vitiligo occurs at similar prevalence across all skin tones, but is far more visible and psychologically impactful in darker-skinned individuals. This drives higher rates of diagnosis and treatment-seeking in populations with darker complexions.

riskFactors.factor: Friction and trauma to the skin
riskFactors.description: Koebner phenomenon is well-documented in vitiligo — mechanical friction (tight clothing, shoe friction, rubbing), cuts, burns, and surgical scars can trigger new depigmented patches. Patients should minimize unnecessary skin trauma.

Occupational or cosmetic exposure to phenolic compounds (hydroquinone, catechols, resorcinol, paratertiary butyl phenol) can trigger vitiligo through melanocyte toxicity. Common sources include adhesives, rubber products, disinfectants, and hair dyes.
Sunburn, particularly blistering sunburns, is a documented trigger for new vitiligo patches and may worsen existing depigmentation. Consistent sun protection is recommended.

affectedDemographics: Vitiligo affects approximately 1% of the global population — an estimated 70-80 million people worldwide
Prevalence is similar across all ethnicities and geographic regions, though the cosmetic impact varies significantly by skin tone
Men and women are affected equally, though women are more likely to seek treatment, creating an apparent female predominance in clinical populations
Half of patients develop vitiligo before age 30; the condition can present at any age from infancy to old age, though onset after age 50 is uncommon
Segmental vitiligo accounts for 5-15% of cases and typically presents in childhood or early adolescence
Up to 20-30% of patients have a first-degree relative with vitiligo, and 15-20% have at least one other autoimmune condition
diagnosisOverview: Vitiligo diagnosis is primarily clinical — the characteristic appearance of well-demarcated, completely depigmented (chalk-white) macules and patches is highly distinctive. A Wood lamp examination (UV light at 365nm) is useful to delineate depigmented areas, particularly in fair-skinned individuals where depigmentation may be subtle without the lamp. Wood lamp also distinguishes vitiligo from other hypopigmented disorders (tinea corporis shows fluorescence, post-inflammatory hypopigmentation shows partial pigmentation). A thorough history includes onset pattern, progression rate, prior treatments, family history of vitiligo and autoimmune disease, and triggering factors. A skin biopsy is not routinely needed but shows complete absence of melanocytes on immunohistochemistry in active lesions. All newly diagnosed patients should be screened for associated autoimmune conditions: thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), thyroid antibodies (anti-TPO, anti-Tg), fasting glucose, and in appropriate clinical contexts, anti-parietal cell and anti-intrinsic factor antibodies.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A handheld UV lamp (365nm) highlights depigmented areas more clearly than visible light — in fair-skinned individuals, vitiligo patches appear bright blue-white under Wood lamp, distinguishing them from normal skin and from partial hypopigmentation. Also useful for assessing response to treatment and detecting subclinical depigmentation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 1-minute non-invasive examination in a darkened room. The clinician holds the Wood lamp close to the skin and examines affected areas. The contrast between depigmented and normal skin is accentuated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's thyroiditis and Graves' disease) is the most common comorbidity in vitiligo patients, affecting 10-30% of patients. TSH, free T4, anti-thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies, and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies should be checked at diagnosis and periodically monitored.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results typically available within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is associated with vitiligo through shared autoimmune mechanisms. Screening is particularly important in pediatric-onset vitiligo, where the association with type 1 diabetes is strongest.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Simple blood test; results within 1 day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Not routinely required. Reserved for diagnostically challenging cases where the clinical appearance is atypical. Histology shows complete absence of melanocytes in fully depigmented lesions, with a subepidermal lymphocytic infiltrate at the lesion margins.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Punch biopsy under local anesthesia; results in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count and autoimmune panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: In patients with clinical signs suggesting broader autoimmune involvement, screening for pernicious anemia (anti-parietal cell antibodies, vitamin B12), Addison's disease, or other autoimmune endocrinopathies may be warranted.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for multiple autoantibody tests; results within 1 week.

treatmentOverview: Treatment for vitiligo follows a stepwise approach based on disease activity, extent, and patient age. For localized, early-stage disease, topical therapies are first-line: high-potency topical corticosteroids (clobetasol propionate) or topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) applied to affected areas. Phototherapy is the cornerstone of treatment for generalized or rapidly progressive vitiligo — narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) is the preferred modality, administered 2-3 times weekly in a dermatology office or home phototherapy unit, with response typically visible at 3-4 months and optimal results at 9-12 months. For refractory or extensive disease, systemic approaches include oral mini-pulse corticosteroids (to halt active depigmentation), JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, ruxolitinib — recently FDA-approved or in trials for vitiligo), and methotrexate. Surgical melanocyte transfer (split-thickness skin grafting, suction blister epidermal grafting, or melanocyte-keratinocyte suspension transplantation) is appropriate for stable, refractory, segmental vitiligo. For patients with extensive disease who do not repigment satisfactorily, depigmentation of remaining normal skin with monobenzyl ether of hydroquinone is an option. All patients benefit from sun protection and psychological support.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical high-potency corticosteroid (Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line topical therapy for localized vitiligo. High-potency steroids suppress local immune activity and may promote melanocyte migration from hair follicles. Applied once daily to affected areas for limited periods (2-4 weeks on, 1-2 weeks off) to avoid skin atrophy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves repigmentation in 30-50% of patients with localized disease; best results on the face and trunk. Face and intertriginous areas respond better than hands and feet.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors — Tacrolimus 0.03% or 0.1% ointment
medicalTreatments.description: First-line alternative to topical steroids, particularly for facial and intertriginous vitiligo where steroid atrophy is a concern. Tacrolimus blocks T-cell activation and cytokine production in the skin, suppressing the autoimmune attack on melanocytes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable efficacy to high-potency steroids for facial vitiligo (45-60% achieve some repigmentation). Does not cause skin atrophy. No systemic immunosuppression. Approved for children as young as 2 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) phototherapy
medicalTreatments.description: The gold standard treatment for generalized, progressive, or extensive vitiligo. NB-UVB (311nm) is administered 2-3 times weekly, with initial exposures of seconds to minutes and gradual dose escalation based on erythema response. Home phototherapy units are available for maintenance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The most effective repigmentation treatment — 60-75% of patients achieve at least 75% repigmentation. Best response on the face, neck, and trunk (80-90% response); worst response on acral areas (hands and feet, 30-40%). Visible response at 3-4 months; optimal at 9-12 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical JAK inhibitors — Tofacitinib 2% or Ruxolitinib 1.5% cream
medicalTreatments.description: Janus kinase inhibitors block the JAK-STAT signaling pathway critical for IFN-gamma-driven melanocyte destruction in vitiligo. Topical formulations provide local immunosuppression without systemic effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Ruxolitinib cream (Opzelura) is FDA-approved for vitiligo. Clinical trials show 30-40% of patients achieve at least 75% repigmentation at week 24. Particularly effective on the face. Can be combined with NB-UVB for synergistic effect.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic JAK inhibitors — Tofacitinib, Ruxolitinib (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: For extensive, refractory vitiligo not responding to topical therapy and phototherapy. Oral JAK inhibitors provide systemic immunomodulation targeting the IFN-gamma/CD8+ T cell axis of vitiligo pathogenesis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Promising early data from case series and small trials — 50-70% of patients achieve significant repigmentation. Rapid action (visible at 2-3 months). Currently used off-label; ongoing trials for FDA approval in vitiligo.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral mini-pulse corticosteroid therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose oral corticosteroids (dexamethasone 2.5-5mg or betamethasone 5mg) administered twice weekly to halt active depigmentation — particularly useful during the rapid progression phase. This approach achieves immunosuppression without the side effects of daily corticosteroid use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Halts disease progression in 80-90% of active vitiligo patients within 4-6 weeks. Can be used as an adjunct to phototherapy to convert active disease to stable disease before repigmentation therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Depigmentation therapy — Monobenzyl ether of hydroquinone (monobenzone) 20%
medicalTreatments.description: For patients with extensive vitiligo (>50-80% body surface area) who have not responded to repigmentation therapies and prefer a uniform appearance. Permanently and irreversibly destroys remaining melanocytes, resulting in complete depigmentation of the entire body.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in 90%+ of patients who complete the course. Requires 9-12 months of application to remaining pigmented areas. The depigmentation is permanent — patients must commit to strict sun protection for the rest of their lives.

surgicalOptions.name: Melanocyte-keratinocyte suspension transplantation (MKST)
surgicalOptions.description: The gold standard surgical technique for stable vitiligo. A thin split-thickness skin graft is harvested from a pigmented donor site, processed to create a suspension of melanocytes and keratinocytes, and applied to dermabraded recipient vitiligo skin.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Repigmentation rates of 70-90% in stable non-segmental and segmental vitiligo. Excellent cosmetic match. Best results in segmental vitiligo and stable non-segmental vitiligo with good repigmentation potential.

surgicalOptions.name: Suction blister epidermal grafting
surgicalOptions.description: Melanocyte-rich epidermis is harvested by applying suction to normal-pigmented skin, creating blisters at the dermal-epidermal junction. The blister roofs (epidermal grafts) are then transferred to dermabraded vitiligo areas.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Repigmentation rates of 70-85%. Minimally invasive with minimal scarring at the donor site. Less melanocyte yield than split-thickness grafting but excellent cosmetic results.

surgicalOptions.name: Split-thickness skin grafting
surgicalOptions.description: Thin sheets of pigmented skin are harvested with a dermatome and transplanted to dermabraded vitiligo recipient sites. Provides the highest melanocyte yield of any technique but requires surgical expertise and creates a donor site wound.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Repigmentation rates of 80-95% in stable vitiligo. Best for small to medium-sized lesions. Risk of cobblestone appearance and donor site scarring.

surgicalOptions.name: Punch grafting
surgicalOptions.description: Small (2-4mm) full-thickness punch biopsies of pigmented skin are transplanted into punch holes created in depigmented skin. Simple technique but produces a cobblestone appearance and is largely superseded by more sophisticated methods.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Repigmentation rates of 50-60% at the graft site; cobblestone appearance limits cosmetic acceptability. Largely historical.

alternativeTreatments.name: Camouflage cosmetics and self-tanners
alternativeTreatments.description: Waterproof cover creams (Dermablend, Cover FX, Korean brand products) and self-tanning products (dihydroxyacetone/DHA) provide temporary cosmetic coverage for depigmented patches. These are important quality-of-life tools, particularly for visible areas like the face and hands.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Immediate cosmetic benefit. Self-tanners produce results lasting 5-7 days per application. Cover creams require daily application but provide excellent camouflage.

alternativeTreatments.name: Psychological counseling and support groups
alternativeTreatments.description: Vitiligo causes significant psychological burden including depression, social anxiety, and reduced self-esteem — particularly in cultures where depigmentation is stigmatized. Cognitive behavioral therapy, support groups, and patient education organizations (Global Vitiligo Foundation) provide essential support.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Psychological support does not treat the skin but significantly improves coping, body image, and quality of life. Recommended for all patients with moderate-to-severe disease impact.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pseudocatalase cream and topical antioxidants
alternativeTreatments.description: Topical formulations containing calcium, copper sulfate, and antioxidant compounds aim to reduce oxidative stress in vitiligo skin. Early studies showed promise but replication has been inconsistent.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Modest evidence in early-stage disease; used as adjunctive therapy rather than standalone treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should be seen for: any new depigmented patches (for accurate diagnosis); rapidly progressive vitiligo (to initiate treatment to halt progression); vitiligo affecting the face, hands, or other cosmetically sensitive areas (early treatment is most effective); children with vitiligo (specialized pediatric dermatology approach); segmental vitiligo in children (early surgical intervention is often indicated); and any patient with significant psychological distress. General practitioners can provide initial diagnosis and referral but typically do not manage active treatment beyond initial topical therapy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified dermatologist with expertise in pigmentary disorders — not all dermatologists focus on vitiligo specifically. Ask about their experience with phototherapy (NB-UVB), topical immunomodulators, and surgical melanocyte transfer. A vitiligo specialist should offer the full range of medical and surgical options and be comfortable with JAK inhibitors (a newer treatment class). For children with vitiligo, a pediatric dermatologist with specific expertise is optimal. Co-management with an endocrinologist for patients with associated autoimmune thyroid disease or diabetes is important.
preventionStrategies: Avoid skin trauma and friction — tight clothing, vigorous rubbing, and injuries can trigger new vitiligo patches (Koebner phenomenon); use soft fabrics and avoid unnecessary skin injury

Minimize chemical exposure — avoid skin contact with phenolic compounds (found in adhesives, rubber products, certain hair dyes, and industrial chemicals); perform patch testing if occupational exposure is suspected
Use broad-spectrum sunscreen daily — sun protection prevents sunburn in normal skin, reducing the contrast between depigmented and pigmented skin; also reduces risk of sunburn-triggered new patches
Promptly treat skin injuries — immediate wound care and monitoring of healing after cuts, scrapes, or surgical procedures
Stress management — emotional stress is a reported trigger for vitiligo flares; stress reduction techniques may reduce flare frequency
Regular monitoring — patients with one autoimmune disease should be screened for others; annual thyroid function testing is recommended for all vitiligo patients
lifestyleModifications: Use sunscreen (SPF 30+) on all exposed skin daily — this reduces tanning contrast between affected and unaffected areas, making patches less visible and preventing sunburn in depigmented skin
Apply cosmetic camouflage products — concealers and foundations specifically formulated for vitiligo provide excellent coverage; waterproof options are available for long-lasting coverage
Avoid harsh skin treatments — chemical peels, laser hair removal, and aggressive exfoliation on vitiligo-prone skin can trigger new patches
Choose gentle fabrics — soft cotton and silk reduce friction; avoid wool and rough synthetic materials that cause skin irritation
Use mild, fragrance-free laundry detergents — residues on clothing can cause contact irritation in sensitive skin

recommended: Antioxidant-rich foods — fruits and vegetables high in vitamins C, E, and beta-carotene support skin health and may reduce oxidative stress (berries, leafy greens, carrots, tomatoes)
Vitamin D supplementation — vitiligo patients may have lower vitamin D levels; supplementation is commonly recommended, particularly in northern latitudes with limited sun exposure
B12 and folate supplementation — some studies show B12 and folate deficiency in vitiligo patients; supplementation may support repigmentation in deficient individuals
Zinc-rich foods — zinc is involved in melanin synthesis; oysters, pumpkin seeds, and legumes are good sources
Iron-rich foods — iron deficiency is common in vitiligo and may impair melanocyte function; lean meats, beans, and fortified cereals
avoid: Gluten — a subset of vitiligo patients (particularly those with other autoimmune conditions) may have occult celiac disease; gluten sensitivity is not universal but worth investigating if GI symptoms are present
Excessive alcohol — alcohol may worsen vitiligo through oxidative stress mechanisms; moderate consumption is generally considered safe
Processed foods high in saturated fats — pro-inflammatory diet may exacerbate autoimmune activity; processed meats, fried foods, and sugary snacks should be minimized

Foods containing phenolic compounds — in patients with chemical-induced or contact vitiligo, foods high in phenolic compounds (certain fruits, vegetables, spices) may theoretically contribute to melanocyte stress; evidence is limited

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is beneficial and recommended for vitiligo patients — there is no specific contraindication. However, patients should be aware of the following: avoid exercises that cause significant friction or trauma to the skin (prolonged rowing, horse riding, aggressive contact sports) as skin injury can trigger Koebner phenomenon; use protective padding under sports equipment; choose soft, moisture-wicking fabrics to reduce chafing; shower promptly after exercise to remove sweat and bacteria from skin; and wear sunscreen during outdoor exercise as depigmented skin burns easily. Swimming is an excellent low-friction option, though pool chlorine may be drying — apply moisturizer after swimming.
dailyManagement: Apply topical tacrolimus or corticosteroid as prescribed — consistency is critical for efficacy; typically once or twice daily to affected areas
Attend NB-UVB phototherapy sessions 2-3 times weekly (or use home unit as prescribed) — treatment is most effective when administered consistently without long gaps
Apply broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen every morning — depigmented skin burns easily and burns can trigger new patches
Perform monthly self-skin examinations — photograph affected areas with a consistent technique (same lighting, same distance) to track progression or response to treatment

Monitor for signs of disease activity — new patches appearing, existing patches expanding, or whitening of hair in previously pigmented areas indicate active disease requiring prompt dermatologist review
prognosis: Vitiligo is a chronic, relapsing condition with no guaranteed cure. However, with modern treatment approaches, the majority of patients can achieve meaningful repigmentation — 60-75% of patients treated with NB-UVB achieve at least 75% repigmentation of treated areas. The face, neck, and trunk respond best (80-90% response); hands and feet respond poorly (30-40%). Segmental vitiligo, once stabilized, has the best prognosis for surgical repigmentation. Non-segmental vitiligo tends to be progressive and relapsing — it rarely spontaneously repigments and typically requires ongoing treatment. The earlier treatment is initiated, the better the outcome, as recent depigmentation responds better than long-standing stable patches. Patients should understand that vitiligo is controllable but may require long-term or lifelong maintenance therapy.
recoveryTimeline: With NB-UVB phototherapy: initial repigmentation (small perifollicular pigment dots) appears at 3-4 months; moderate repigmentation at 6-9 months; maximal repigmentation at 12-18 months. With topical therapy alone: initial response at 4-6 months, maximal at 9-12 months. With JAK inhibitors (topical or oral): faster initial response at 6-8 weeks, with progressive improvement over 6-12 months. With surgical melanocyte transfer: repigmentation begins within 4-6 weeks at graft sites; cosmetically significant at 3-6 months.
complications: Permanent depigmentation — without treatment, vitiligo patches do not spontaneously repigment and persist indefinitely. The earlier treatment is initiated, the better the chance of repigmentation.

Sunburn and photodamage — depigmented skin lacks melanin and is extremely susceptible to sunburn and UV damage. Regular sunscreen use is mandatory to prevent acute burns and long-term skin cancer risk.

Psychological and social impact — vitiligo causes significant depression, social anxiety, low self-esteem, and reduced quality of life. In cultures with significant stigma around depigmentation, patients experience discrimination and relationship difficulties. Clinical depression and anxiety disorders are more prevalent in vitiligo patients.

Associated autoimmune diseases — 15-20% of vitiligo patients have at least one other autoimmune condition, most commonly autoimmune thyroid disease. Undiagnosed thyroid disease can cause fatigue, weight changes, and cardiac complications if untreated.
Ocular abnormalities — some vitiligo patients have retinal pigment epithelium changes or uveitis; baseline ophthalmologic examination is recommended in patients with periorbital involvement.

Cosmetic disfigurement — progressive disease, particularly acrofacial vitiligo affecting the hands and face, causes significant cosmetic disfigurement that is difficult to camouflage and can profoundly affect self-image and social functioning.

supportResources.name: Global Vitiligo Foundation
supportResources.description: US-based nonprofit providing patient education, support resources, physician finder, research updates, and community connection for vitiligo patients.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Vitiligo Patient Resource Center
supportResources.description: Evidence-based information from the largest dermatologic professional organization, including treatment options and finding a board-certified dermatologist.

supportResources.name: NICE CKS: Vitiligo. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2023.
supportResources.description: UK clinical knowledge summary providing evidence-based management guidance for clinicians and patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pityriasis Alba
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pityriasis alba presents with hypopigmented (not depigmented — some pigment remains) scaly patches on the face and upper arms, typically in children. The borders are poorly defined and the skin feels dry. No complete absence of pigment as in vitiligo. Self-resolves over months.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tinea Corporis (Ringworm)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tinea presents with annular (ring-shaped) scaly plaques with raised, red borders and central clearing. Vitiligo patches are flat, non-scaly, and completely depigmented. KOH prep shows fungal elements in tinea.

confusedWithConditions.name: Post-Inflammatory Hypopigmentation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypopigmentation following skin inflammation (eczema, psoriasis, burns, laser treatments) leaves skin lighter than normal but not completely depigmented. History of preceding inflammation is present. Melanocytes are present but underactive.

confusedWithConditions.name: Piebaldism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Piebaldism is a congenital, hereditary condition present from birth — stable, non-progressive depigmented patches typically on the forehead (white forelock), central scalp, anterior chest, and mid-abdomen. Family history is usually positive. Vitiligo is acquired and progressive.

relatedConditions.name: Alopecia Areata
relatedConditions.relevance: Alopecia areata and vitiligo share autoimmune pathogenesis and frequently co-occur — both involve T-cell-mediated destruction of a specific cell type (melanocytes in vitiligo, hair follicles in alopecia). The same genetic loci (HLA-DRB1, PTPN22) are implicated in both conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Autoimmune Thyroid Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune thyroid disease is the most common comorbidity of vitiligo — 10-30% of vitiligo patients have Hashimoto's or Graves' disease. All vitiligo patients should have thyroid function screening.

relatedConditions.name: Piebaldism
relatedConditions.relevance: Congenital depigmentation disorder that is sometimes confused with vitiligo at birth. Stable and non-progressive, unlike acquired progressive vitiligo.

faqs.question: Is vitiligo hereditary?
faqs.answer: Vitiligo has a significant genetic component — approximately 20-30% of patients have a first-degree relative with the condition, and over 40 genetic loci have been associated with vitiligo risk. However, it is not strictly hereditary in the Mendelian sense — having a parent with vitiligo increases your risk from 1% to approximately 6-22%, but most people with a family history do not develop it. Environmental triggers (trauma, stress, chemicals) are required to unmask the genetic predisposition.

faqs.question: What is the difference between segmental and non-segmental vitiligo?
faqs.answer: Non-segmental vitiligo (NSV) is the most common form, affecting 80-90% of patients. It causes bilateral, symmetric depigmented patches on the face, neck, trunk, and extremities, and follows a relapsing-remitting course with unpredictable spread. Segmental vitiligo (SV) affects 5-15% of patients, causing unilateral depigmentation in a dermatomal or band-like pattern. SV appears earlier in life, progresses rapidly for 6-12 months then stabilizes permanently, and is less associated with other autoimmune diseases. Treatment differs — SV responds well to surgical melanocyte transfer once stable.

faqs.question: Can vitiligo be treated?
faqs.answer: Yes — vitiligo is highly treatable, though not always curable. The first goal is to halt active depigmentation using topical steroids, calcineurin inhibitors, or oral mini-pulse corticosteroids. The second goal is repigmentation using NB-UVB phototherapy (the most effective treatment, 60-75% achieve 75% repigmentation), topical JAK inhibitors, or in refractory cases, surgical melanocyte transfer. For patients with extensive disease who do not repigment, depigmentation of remaining skin is an option.

faqs.question: Does vitiligo spread?
faqs.answer: Vitiligo has an unpredictable natural course. Some patients have stable disease with patches that do not change for years; others have rapidly progressive disease with new patches appearing monthly. Non-segmental vitiligo typically follows a chronic, progressive course without treatment. Segmental vitiligo progresses rapidly for 6-12 months then stabilizes. Treatment halts progression in most patients. Common triggers for new patches include skin trauma (Koebner phenomenon), sunburn, emotional stress, and chemical exposure.

faqs.question: What is the Koebner phenomenon in vitiligo?
faqs.answer: The Koebner phenomenon is the development of new vitiligo patches at sites of skin trauma — cuts, scrapes, burns, friction, surgical wounds, tattoos, or even vigorous rubbing. This occurs because skin injury triggers an inflammatory response that can recruit autoreactive T cells to the wound site, where they attack melanocytes. Patients with vitiligo should minimize unnecessary skin trauma and protect areas prone to friction.

faqs.question: Is vitiligo associated with other diseases?
faqs.answer: Yes — vitiligo is strongly associated with other autoimmune diseases, particularly autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's and Graves' disease), which affects 10-30% of vitiligo patients. Other associated conditions include type 1 diabetes, pernicious anemia, Addison's disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and alopecia areata. All newly diagnosed vitiligo patients should be screened for thyroid disease and diabetes.

faqs.question: Does diet affect vitiligo?
faqs.answer: Diet does not cause or cure vitiligo, but certain dietary factors may be helpful. Antioxidant-rich foods (fruits and vegetables) may help reduce oxidative stress in the skin. Some patients have benefited from vitamin D, B12, and folate supplementation if deficient. Zinc and iron are important for melanin synthesis. There is no strong evidence for restrictive diets in vitiligo, though patients with concurrent celiac disease should follow a gluten-free diet.

faqs.question: Can vitiligo be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no universally guaranteed cure for vitiligo. However, with modern treatment, many patients achieve near-complete or complete repigmentation that is maintained long-term with minimal maintenance therapy. The face, neck, and trunk respond best. Hands and feet are notoriously difficult to repigment. Early treatment produces better outcomes than delayed treatment. Even when full repigmentation is achieved, lifelong monitoring and occasional maintenance treatment may be needed.

faqs.question: What is surgical treatment for vitiligo?
faqs.answer: Surgical melanocyte transfer is appropriate for stable vitiligo that has not responded to medical therapy. The most common technique is melanocyte-keratinocyte suspension transplantation — a thin skin graft is harvested, processed to separate melanocytes and keratinocytes into a suspension, and applied to dermabraded vitiligo skin. Repigmentation rates are 70-90% in appropriate candidates. Best results in segmental vitiligo and stable non-segmental vitiligo with good repigmentation potential.

faqs.question: Does sun exposure help or worsen vitiligo?
faqs.answer: Controlled sun exposure is beneficial for vitiligo in moderation — UVB is the active wavelength used in medical phototherapy, and controlled NB-UVB treatment is the most effective repigmentation therapy. However, sunburn is harmful — it can worsen existing vitiligo and trigger new patches through the Koebner phenomenon. Depigmented skin has no melanin protection and burns easily. Daily broad-spectrum sunscreen use is mandatory; intentional sunbathing without protection is not recommended.

faqs.question: Is vitiligo contagious?
faqs.answer: No — vitiligo is absolutely not contagious. It cannot be transmitted through physical contact, sharing of objects, blood transfusion, or any other form of exposure. Vitiligo is an autoimmune condition caused by the body's immune system attacking its own melanocytes. It is entirely an individual genetic and immune system matter.

faqs.question: What is the psychological impact of vitiligo?
faqs.answer: Vitiligo has a profound psychological impact — studies consistently show high rates of depression (20-30%), anxiety, and social phobia in vitiligo patients. The visible nature of the condition, cultural stigma around depigmentation in many societies, and the chronic, unpredictable course contribute to psychological distress. Young women and individuals with darker skin tones (where depigmentation is more visible) are particularly affected. Psychological support, counseling, and patient support groups are important components of comprehensive vitiligo care.

metaTitle: Vitiligo: Types, Causes, Treatment & Prognosis | aihealz
metaDescription: Vitiligo: progressive depigmented white patches from autoimmune melanocyte destruction. Learn about types, causes, NB-UVB treatment, JAK inhibitors, surgical options, and psychological impact.

images.altText: Clinical presentation of non-segmental vitiligo showing symmetric depigmented patches on the back
images.caption: Non-segmental vitiligo typically presents with bilateral, symmetric depigmented patches affecting characteristic sites including the back.

images.altText: Depigmented patches on the hands showing classic vitiligo appearance with well-demarcated chalk-white areas
images.caption: The hands are a common site for vitiligo, though acral areas typically respond poorly to repigmentation therapy.

images.altText: Periorificial vitiligo showing depigmentation around the eyes and mouth
images.caption: Periorificial areas (around the eyes, nose, and mouth) are commonly affected in generalized vitiligo and respond well to treatment.
